s1_1: No specific AI use cases identified
General AI interest, no defined use cases
1–2 use cases named but not fully scoped
Specific use cases defined with clear business drivers
Use cases defined with success metrics, priority order, and timeline
s1_2: Informal leadership interest, no formal mandate
Named executive sponsor, no formal mandate
Formal executive mandate with named owner and governance
Board-directed mandate with funding authority and defined timeline
s1_3: No assessment of gap between ambition and current capacity
Informally aware of gap, no structured plan
Gap analysis started, partial remediation plan
Gap analyzed and documented, phased plan in place
Phased roadmap with capacity milestones, resource plan, and sequencing
s1_4: No success metrics defined for any AI initiative
General goals stated, no measurable metrics
Metrics defined for some use cases only
Metrics defined for all priority use cases
Metrics defined, baselined, and tied to governance review cycles
s1_5: No timeline or urgency drivers identified
General urgency acknowledged, no specific timeline
Some timeline anchors exist (e.g., board deadline)
Clear timeline with named milestones and owners
Timeline tied to budget cycle, board commitments, and contract milestones
s2_1: Data required for AI use cases is not available
Data exists but access is unclear or inconsistent
Data available for some use cases, gaps for others
Data available and accessible for all priority use cases
Data pipelines built, access documented, availability SLAs defined
s2_2: No assessment of data quality for AI use cases
Data quality issues known but not quantified
Quality assessed for some domains, issues partially remediated
Data quality assessed and documented for all priority domains
Quality validated with metrics, remediation complete, ongoing monitoring in place
s2_3: No interoperability standards or integration capability in place
Core interoperability in place for some systems
FHIR R4 or equivalent fully deployed across key systems
Full interoperability with certified APIs, tested integrations, and governance
s2_4: Informal data stewardship, no formal governance
Data governance in early development, key policies drafted
Formal data governance with policies, stewards, and oversight body
Mature governance with AI-specific data policies, audit trail, and metrics
s2_5: EHR not configured for AI integration
EHR AI capabilities available but not assessed or activated
Some EHR AI modules assessed or piloting
EHR AI ecosystem evaluated and integration path defined
EHR AI integration active with defined roadmap and vendor alignment
s2_6: No data platform or analytics infrastructure in place
Basic reporting tools only, no enterprise data platform
Data warehouse or lake in place, AI readiness not assessed
Enterprise data platform operational and assessed for AI workloads
Cloud-native data platform with AI/ML pipeline capability validated
s3_1: No dedicated compute for AI workloads
Shared compute only, AI workload capacity unknown
Limited dedicated compute available for pilot use
Dedicated compute provisioned for priority AI workloads
Scalable AI compute with defined capacity plan and cost model
s3_2: Cloud usage is ad hoc, no formal strategy
Cloud strategy drafted, primary provider identified
Formal cloud strategy with defined primary provider and governance
Mature cloud strategy with AI-specific services evaluated and roadmap defined
s3_3: Network not assessed for AI workload requirements
Network baseline exists but AI readiness unknown
Some network upgrades completed, gaps remain
Network assessed and meets AI latency and bandwidth requirements
Network fully optimized for AI with monitoring, redundancy, and capacity plan
s3_4: No integration architecture for AI tools
Point-to-point integrations only, no architecture plan
Integration architecture drafted, key APIs identified
Integration architecture defined with API gateway and standards
Mature integration platform with documented API contracts and testing
s3_5: No scalability or resilience planning for AI
Basic uptime SLAs exist, AI-specific resilience not assessed
Some resilience planning done for priority systems
Scalability and resilience requirements defined for all AI use cases
Fully designed for scale with tested failover, DR plan, and capacity headroom
s4_1: CISO not involved in AI procurement or deployment
CISO involved in some AI decisions but not all
CISO formally reviews all AI procurement and deployment decisions
Documented CISO review process with defined timelines and sign-off requirements
s4_2: No third-party risk management process for AI vendors
Ad hoc vendor security reviews, no formal process
Vendor review process exists but not AI-specific
Formal third-party risk process applied to all AI vendors
AI-specific vendor risk framework with ongoing monitoring and contract requirements
s4_3: No incident response plan relevant to AI systems
General incident response plan exists, AI not addressed
Incident response plan updated to include some AI scenarios
AI-specific incident scenarios included in IR plan and tested
AI incident playbooks tested, breach notification process documented and insurers informed
s4_4: No assessment of AI regulatory compliance requirements
Aware of regulatory landscape, no formal compliance assessment
Partial compliance assessment done for priority use cases
Compliance requirements mapped and addressed for all AI use cases
Ongoing compliance monitoring with legal review, audit trail, and regulatory tracking
s4_5: No requirements defined for how AI vendors handle our data
General data handling expectations, not formally required
Some vendor data handling requirements in place
Formal data handling requirements applied to all AI vendor negotiations
Contractual data handling requirements with audit rights and breach notification
s4_6: No security requirements included in AI vendor contracts
Basic security language in contracts, not AI-specific
Security requirements present in some contracts
Standardized security requirements in all AI vendor contracts
Requirements include model governance, data residency, and penetration test rights
s5_1: No AI governance committee or equivalent exists
Informal AI discussions at leadership level, no formal body
AI governance committee forming or meeting ad hoc
Formal AI governance committee with charter and regular cadence
Mature governance body with clinical, legal, IT, and finance representation and decision authority
s5_2: No process for approving new AI tools
New AI tools adopted informally without structured review
Some review steps in place but process not formalized
Defined approval process with clinical, security, and governance review
End-to-end approval workflow documented, tested, and applied to all AI tools
s5_3: No inventory of AI tools currently in use
Partial awareness of AI tools in use, no formal tracking
Inventory exists for major systems, gaps for departmental tools
Complete inventory of all AI tools with vendor, use case, and risk level
Living inventory with usage metrics, expiration tracking, and governance status
s5_4: No accountability structure for AI performance or harms
Accountability assigned for some AI tools
Named owners with defined accountability for all AI tools in production
Accountability framework with escalation path, board visibility, and performance reporting
s5_5: No monitoring of AI tools after deployment
Ad hoc review of AI performance, no structured monitoring
Monitoring in place for some AI tools
Formal monitoring program covering all production AI tools
Automated monitoring with drift detection, bias review, and performance dashboards
s5_6: No operating model defined for AI
Informal understanding of roles, nothing documented
Operating model drafted for some AI functions
Documented operating model with roles, workflows, and decision rights
Mature operating model with staffing plan, training requirements, and KPIs
s6_1: Clinical leaders not engaged in AI initiative
Some informal clinical interest, no structured engagement
CMIO or clinical informatics involved, broader clinical engagement limited
Clinical leaders formally engaged with defined input into AI use cases
Clinical governance integrated into AI program with co-design and sign-off
s6_2: No change management capacity for technology adoption
Ad hoc change support, no structured capability
Change management resources available but not dedicated to AI
Dedicated change management capability with proven track record
Change management integrated into AI program with adoption metrics and playbook
s6_3: No executive sponsor for AI adoption
Informal executive interest, no named sponsor
Named sponsor exists but engagement is limited
Active executive sponsor with regular engagement and resource authority
C-suite sponsor with board visibility, budget authority, and public commitment
s6_4: No plan for integrating AI into clinical or operational workflows
General awareness of workflow impact, no integration planning
Workflow integration considered for some use cases
Integration plan developed for all priority AI use cases
Integration plans validated with end users and piloted before full deployment
s6_5: Multiple unresolved hard constraints blocking AI adoption
Several constraints identified, no mitigation plan
Some constraints have mitigation plans, others unresolved
All hard constraints identified with owners and mitigation timelines
Constraints tracked, mitigations tested, and constraint burden actively managed
s7_1: No RFP or procurement process exists for technology at this scale
Ad hoc procurement, RFP process informal or inconsistent
RFP process exists but not tailored for AI or at-scale technology
Formal RFP process defined and previously used for comparable technology
Mature RFP process with AI-specific requirements, scoring criteria, and governance approval
s7_2: No defined AI infrastructure requirements before vendor conversations
General requirements discussed but not documented
Comprehensive requirements documented before any vendor engagement
Requirements validated by stakeholders, prioritized, and traceable to use cases
s7_3: No capability to evaluate AI vendors objectively
Vendor evaluation done informally based on demos
Some evaluation criteria defined, not consistently applied
Structured evaluation criteria covering capability, security, and contract terms
Evaluation framework with scoring rubric, reference checks, and proof-of-concept requirements
s7_4: No prior experience procuring AI tools at this scale
Limited experience, one or two small AI purchases
Some AI procurement experience, lessons not formally captured
Documented AI procurement experience with lessons learned applied
Established AI procurement practice with playbook, templates, and vendor management
s7_5: No governance review required before AI procurement
Informal leadership sign-off, no structured governance gate
Some governance review steps in place but inconsistently applied
Defined governance gates required at key procurement milestones
Multi-stage approval required: clinical, legal, security, and finance
s8_1: No budget allocated, approved, or identified
Budget informally discussed but not approved or quantified
Partial budget identified or approved, full funding uncertain
Realistic budget approved and allocated for this initiative
Fully approved multi-year budget with phased spend plan and contingency
s8_2: Budget significantly underestimates the scope of the AI ambition
Some awareness of gap between budget and ambition, no plan
Budget partially aligned, phased approach under discussion
Budget and ambition aligned with documented rationale
Budget, ambition, and timeline fully aligned with board-approved plan
s8_3: No understanding of total cost of ownership for AI
Aware of licensing costs only, infrastructure and operational costs not assessed
Full TCO analysis documented covering licensing, infrastructure, and operations
TCO validated by finance with 3-year projection and cost-per-outcome metrics
s8_4: Significant financial constraints blocking any AI investment
Several financial constraints identified, no mitigation strategy
Some constraints with partial mitigation plans
All financial constraints identified with owners and mitigation timelines
Financial constraints actively managed with scenario planning and board transparency
s8_5: No ROI expectations or value case defined
General expectation of value, no defined ROI framework
ROI expectations discussed but not formally quantified
Documented ROI expectations with defined metrics and timeline
Business case with validated ROI methodology, approved by finance and leadership
